content: I'm thinking about converting my garage into a proper workshop. Right now it's just storage for my woodworking tools and my mountain bike collection, but I want to make it functional for all my hobbies.
That sounds like a great project! Converting a garage can really add value to your home. What kind of hobbies are you planning to accommodate besides woodworking?
Well, I'm an indie game developer, so I need space for my computer setup. I also do photography and drone photography, so I need storage for equipment. Plus I still want room for my mountain bikes.

That's quite a variety of activities! Have you considered a zoned approach, where different areas of the garage are dedicated to specific activities? You could have a woodworking corner, a tech area, and bike storage.
Zoned approach makes sense. I'm 57 and finding that I need better organization as I get older. My current setup is just chaos - tools everywhere, bike parts on the floor.

Organization becomes so important with age. For your woodworking tools, pegboard walls are great for keeping everything visible and accessible. For your tech setup, proper cable management will be key.
Pegboard is a good idea. I also volunteer at an animal shelter on weekends, and I've been thinking about fostering dogs. I'd need to make the space pet-friendly too.
That's wonderful that you volunteer! For a pet-friendly workshop, you'll want durable, easy-to-clean flooring. Epoxy coating over concrete works well and resists scratches from paws.
Epoxy flooring sounds perfect. I'm also thinking about adding a small painting area - I've gotten back into painting recently and need a dedicated space that's away from my computer equipment.
A dedicated painting area is a great idea. You could set up a foldable table near a window for natural light. Just make sure to protect the floor with a drop cloth since paint can be messy.
Good point about the drop cloth. I'm also considering adding some insulation - the garage gets really cold in winter and I'd like to use it year-round.
Insulation is definitely worth the investment for year-round use. You might also want to consider adding a small space heater or portable AC unit depending on your climate.
I'll look into insulation options. One last thing - I need better lighting. The current fluorescent lights are terrible for detail work and photography.
For detailed work and photography, LED shop lights with a high CRI (Color Rendering Index) are ideal. They provide bright, natural-looking light that's perfect for both woodworking and photography.
